verbatimText:
This
study investigates the impact of AI technologies on students’ learning processes and academic performance, with a focus on their perceptions and the challenges associated with AI
adoption.

By addressing these recommendations, educational institutions can ensure that AI
complements rather than replaces traditional teaching methods, ultimately fostering a more
adaptable, efficient, and personalized learning environment that supports the development
of essential skills for future success.

most
students (83.5%) believe that AI improves their learning efficiency
Based on this study, we conclude that AI offers significant benefits, such as personalized learning, improved educational outcomes, and increased student engagement.

However, the rapid information processing and insightful responses provided by AI
challenge traditional learning methods, raising questions about the distinctions between
human learning and machine-based learning.
The integration of Artificial Intelligence (AI) in education has transformed academic learning, offering both opportunities and challenges for students’ development.
3.5% feel that it only limits knowledge acquisition

AI integration in education raises critical questions about its
alignment with established learning theories

Virtual assistants
are the most used AI applications (88.2%), providing support for information retrieval, task
management, and real-time feedback.
The use of AI in education is not without its challenges

The questionnaire consisted of 11 items, categorized into two types of questions
as follows; 7 closed-ended questions designed to quantify students’ responses and identify
patterns, and 4 open-ended questions aimed at capturing detailed qualitative insights into
participants’ experiences, perceptions, and expectations regarding the impact of AI on their
learning.
According to the statistical data obtained, 95.6% of respondents use artificial intelligence technologies in academic activities.

its successful
implementation requires addressing concerns related to accuracy, cognitive disengagement,
and ethical implications.

The study underscores the necessity of a structured framework for AI integration, supported by ethical
guidelines, to maximize benefits while mitigating risks.

Qadir (2023) highlights the risk of students misusing AI tools in dishonest or unauthorized ways, such as using AI-generated content to complete assignments without proper
attribution

AI saves time by quickly synthesizing broad topics upon
request; reduces study hours by 50%;

The study utilized
a mixed-methods design, incorporating both quantitative and qualitative data collection
techniques.

Quantitative data were analyzed using frequency and percentage
calculations, while qualitative responses were subjected to thematic analysis, incorporating
both vertical (individual responses) and horizontal (cross-dataset) approaches to ensure
comprehensive theme identification.

systems have been shown to provide personalized feedback and support, improving student engagement and learning outcomes (Luckin et al., 2016)

Studies have shown that students in personalized learning environments exhibit improved
self-efficacy and a more positive attitude toward their education (Johnson & Smith, 2019).

Students widely noted that AI tools reduce the
time spent on tasks like researching, processing information, and automating repetitive
activities.

The current study was conducted in line with the ethical
standards for research involving human subjects, following approval from our University Ethics
Committee.

Regarding the main types of AI used in academic activities, 88.2% of respondents
use virtual assistants (e.g., ChatGPT, Siri, Google Assistant, etc.)

Most students (57.6%) use
them weekly, suggesting that these tools have become an integral part of the educational
process, assisting with homework, projects, and knowledge enhancement.

AI helps us improve our relationships with classmates and enhances the
learning process;

However, it also presents challenges, such as over-reliance on technology, diminished
critical thinking, and the risk of academic fraud.

AI-powered platforms, such as adaptive learning systems, have been
shown to enhance student engagement and performance by providing real-time feedback
and customized learning pathways (Luckin et al., 2016; Zawacki-Richter et al., 2019).

Data were collected using a self-administered questionnaire distributed via Google
Forms

Informed consent was waived for this study because no personal
data were collected from the students, such as first and last names, email addresses, phone numbers,
or any other type of information that could lead to their identification.

using AI-based technologies enhances their educational experience

Longitudinal research: Future studies should adopt longitudinal designs with larger,
more diverse samples to better understand the long-term effects of AI on academic outcomes and career development.

The sample consisted of 85 second-year students, purposefully selected from the
Aerospace and Medical Engineering programs

Using purposive sampling, 85 participants were selected
to ensure relevance.

A balanced approach is essential to ensure equitable, effective,
and responsible learning experiences in AI-enhanced educational environments.

The raw data supporting the conclusions of this article can be made
available by the authors upon request.

AI’ s ability to provide summaries, find relevant information, and perform
complex calculations allows learners to focus more on critical thinking and understanding.

A considerable
percentage, 18.8%, use AI daily, indicating a higher reliance on these technologies, possibly
because they consider them essential for learning, whether through virtual assistants,
educational platforms, or other AI-based tools.

The primary concern
among users is the reliability of data provided by AI tools.

Given the small sample size and its
focus on specific academic programs, the findings should be considered preliminary and
exploratory. They cannot be generalized to all students within these programs or to those
in other fields.

The findings reveal that AI offers significant benefits,
including personalized learning, improved academic outcomes, and enhanced student
engagement.

82.4% of students think that using AI contributes to enhancing their academic
performance

Only 1.2% of students stated that they do not use AI tools at
all, confirming that AI has become an almost indispensable resource in education.

adaptive learning
platforms leverage AI to tailor educational content to individual learners’ needs, promoting
more effective and efficient learning experiences (Holmes et al., 2019)
The results show that 95.6% of respondents use AI in their academic activities, underscoring the deep integration of this technology into modern education.

However, challenges such as over-reliance on AI, diminished critical thinking
skills, data privacy risks, and academic dishonesty were also identified.

11 items as follows: seven closed-ended questions assessing perceptions, usage, and the
effectiveness of AI tools; and four open-ended questions exploring experiences, expectations, and concerns.

statement: The current study was conducted in line with the ethical standards for research involving human subjects, following approval from our University Ethics Committee.

Educational institutions can ensure that AI complements rather than replaces traditional teaching methods, fostering a more adaptable, efficient, and personalized learning environment that supports the development of essential skills for future success.

structure.outcome: more adaptable, efficient, and personalized learning environment supporting essential skills

statement: 15.3% of students believe that implementing AI does not bring significant changes in academic performance

AI integration in education provides substantial benefits such as personalized learning, increased engagement and efficiency, but also introduces notable challenges including over‑reliance, accuracy concerns, and threats to critical thinking; therefore a balanced, ethically‑guided framework is required.
A structured framework for AI integration, supported by ethical guidelines, is necessary to maximize benefits while mitigating risks.

The questionnaire consisted of 11 items, categorized into two types of questions as follows; 7 closed-ended questions designed to quantify students’ responses and identify patterns, and 4 open-ended questions aimed at capturing detailed qualitative insights into participants’ experiences, perceptions, and expectations regarding the impact of AI on their learning.
Future studies should adopt longitudinal designs with larger, more diverse samples to better understand the long-term effects of AI on academic outcomes and career development.

Triangulation further supported the robustness of our findings by ensuring that the identified themes held true across diverse student groups.
AI helps us improve our relationships with classmates and enhances the learning process.

statement: 95.6% of respondents use artificial intelligence technologies in academic activities.

structure.outcome: use of AI technologies in academic activities

statement: Students widely noted that AI tools reduce the time spent on tasks like researching, processing information, and automating repetitive activities.

warrant: Student‑reported concerns about misuse, inaccurate outputs, reduced critical thinking, privacy, and over‑reliance constitute concrete challenges associated with AI use in education.
defeaters: If mitigation strategies (e.g., training, verification protocols) effectively eliminate these concerns

warrant: Triangulation of quantitative and qualitative data enhances the credibility of the study’s findings about AI’s impact.
defeaters: If triangulation was performed on a limited set of themes, reducing its protective effect

warrant: Methodological limitations (small, purposive sample, lack of generalizability) weaken confidence in the claim that AI integration has broadly transformed academic learning.
defeaters: If subsequent studies with larger, more diverse samples replicate the findings

warrant: When both notable benefits and serious risks are present, a structured framework with ethical guidelines is a necessary precondition for responsible implementation.
defeaters: If the benefits overwhelmingly outweigh the risks, a formal framework may be deemed unnecessary

warrant: The primary concern expressed by nearly half of the participants, incorrect or imprecise AI answers, directly evidences a key challenge of AI integration.
defeaters: If AI systems improve accuracy to a level where such concerns become negligible

warrant: Adherence to ethical standards and institutional review underscores the necessity of ethical guidelines in AI integration frameworks.
defeaters: If ethical oversight is superficial and does not translate into concrete practice

warrant: High percentages of students reporting enhanced experience, performance, and efficiency provide quantitative backing for the claim that AI delivers educational benefits.
defeaters: If respondents’ positive attitudes are driven by novelty effects rather than sustained outcomes

warrant: The value statement that AI offers significant benefits aligns with the empirical evidence of personalized learning, improved outcomes, and higher engagement.
defeaters: If the value claim is interpreted as normative rather than descriptive, limiting its evidential weight

warrant: Empirical evidence of improved self‑efficacy, engagement, time‑saving, and collaborative benefits demonstrates that AI offers significant advantages in learning contexts.
defeaters: If the reported improvements are limited to the specific sample or are influenced by self‑report bias

warrant: The overarching observation that AI has transformed learning while presenting opportunities and challenges explains why a balanced, ethical approach is being advocated.
defeaters: If the transformation is superficial or limited to specific disciplines

warrant: The call for longitudinal, larger‑scale research indicates that current evidence is insufficient to firmly assert a lasting transformation caused by AI.
defeaters: If the present cross‑sectional data are corroborated by other robust investigations

rationale: The listed challenges (over‑reliance, diminished critical thinking, privacy risks, academic dishonesty) are derived from self‑reported concerns, not from observed incidents. The source does not indicate that these concerns have manifested as actual harms, so the qualification that they are perceived rather than proven is omitted.
suggestedRemedy: Add a qualifier such as "perceived" or "self‑reported" to indicate that the challenges are based on participant perceptions.

rationale: The claim that AI offers "significant benefits" (personalized learning, improved outcomes, enhanced engagement) is based on data from a purposive sample of 85 students from two engineering programs, which the authors themselves note is not generalizable. Extrapolating to all educational contexts exceeds the scope of the evidence.
suggestedRemedy: Qualify the claim to reflect the limited sample or provide additional data from broader, more diverse populations.

rationale: The claim that AI‑powered platforms have been shown to enhance engagement and performance lacks any cited study or data; no source is provided to substantiate the statement.
suggestedRemedy: Cite peer‑reviewed research that directly measures the impact of adaptive learning systems on engagement and performance, or remove the claim.

rationale: Inference [8] asserts that triangulation of quantitative and qualitative data enhances credibility, yet both data types originate from the same questionnaire administered to the same participants, sharing the same sampling bias and measurement errors, thus not providing independent corroboration.
suggestedRemedy: Obtain qualitative data from a distinct method (e.g., interviews, focus groups) or acknowledge the limitation of using a single instrument for triangulation.

rationale: Inference [0] treats participants' self‑reported improvements in self‑efficacy, engagement, and time‑saving as empirical evidence that AI provides "significant advantages". Perception does not equal objective performance improvement, making the inference logically unsupported.
suggestedRemedy: Support the inference with objective performance metrics or reframe it as a perception‑based observation.

rationale: Both describe excessive reliance on AI reducing critical thinking and independent problem‑solving.
Both state that 95.6% of respondents use AI in academic activities.
[63] lists a subset of challenges (over‑reliance, diminished thinking, academic fraud) omitting data‑privacy risks present in [6].
[53] states information is often incorrect and needs verification, while [41] reports the proportion of students worried about such inaccuracies.
[41] provides a concrete 48.2% figure of concern about incorrect answers, while [51] states the primary concern is reliability in general.
Both indicate that 1.2% of students do not use AI tools at all.
[22] provides a detailed breakdown of the 11‑item questionnaire, making it a more specific description than [3].
[7] proposes a structured framework and ethical guidelines; [8] enumerates concerns to be addressed, focusing on different implementation aspects.
[56] describes a specific personal effect (reduced willingness to ask questions) whereas [6] lists broader over‑reliance challenges.
[14] mentions only challenges, while [0] includes both opportunities and challenges.
Both claim AI improves engagement and outcomes, but refer to different technologies (adaptive learning systems vs intelligent tutoring systems).
[8] lists concerns about accuracy, cognitive disengagement, and ethics; [6] lists over‑reliance, critical‑thinking decline, privacy, and dishonesty, overlapping but distinct challenge sets.
[31] reflects students’ belief that AI enhances the educational experience, while [34] measures belief that AI improves academic performance; the scopes differ (experience vs performance).
[50] quantifies a 50% reduction in study hours, a more specific claim than the general time‑saving noted in [46].
Both give the same proportion (48.2%) of students concerned about inaccurate AI‑generated content.
Both list the same benefits: personalized learning, improved outcomes, and increased engagement.
[19] specifies the sample as 85 second‑year engineering students, whereas [2] only notes 85 purposively selected participants.
Both report that 88.2% of respondents use virtual assistants.
[38] reports a majority believing AI improves learning efficiency; [39] reports a minority disagreeing, both can coexist as differing student opinions.
Both discuss AI reducing time on tasks; [47] additionally highlights how this enables deeper critical thinking.
[11] emphasizes feedback and performance; [17] focuses on content tailoring, so they address different aspects of AI‑driven platforms.

statement: AI is potentially harmful for academic integrity because it may encourage cheating, as it makes tasks too easy.
attemptedPassageText: AI is potentially harmful for academic integrity because it may encourage cheating, as it makes tasks too easy;

statement: 16.5% of students worry about becoming overly dependent on technology due to AI
attemptedPassageText: the risk of becoming overly dependent on technology (16.5%)

statement: The research involved second‑year students who had direct experience with AI‑enhanced learning environments.
attemptedPassageText: Conducted at the National University of Science and Technology POLITEHNICA Bucharest, this research involved second-year students who had direct experience with AI‑enhanced learning environments.
